tweets:
I was building a tabs component with 20 props when I discovered compound components.

No config object. No render props. Just components you compose. Here's what changed for me (thread):

1/ What are compound components?

Multiple components that work together and share implicit state. Parent owns state via context. Children consume it. No prop drilling.

<Tabs><Tabs.Trigger value="a">Tab A</Tabs.Trigger><Tabs.Content value="a">Content</Tabs.Content></Tabs>

2/ How they share state

React Context. Parent creates it, provides value. Children use useContext. The active tab, open state, selected value—all flow implicitly. You never pass activeTab to every child.

3/ When to use them

• Multi-part UI with shared state (tabs, accordions, selects)
• You want max flexibility (reorder, add, omit parts)
• The API would otherwise explode with 10+ props

Don't use for simple Button or Input. Use props.

4/ The trade-off

Compound components = flexible, composable, no prop explosion.

But: more verbose usage, steeper learning curve. Radix UI and Headless UI use this pattern. Study their APIs.

Full post: building Tabs, Accordion, and Select from scratch with the compound pattern. Link in bio.

Bookmark this thread if you're designing component APIs.
